title: A Simple Modern Desk
summary: A parsons-style desk with chunky built-up legs and two soft-close-ready drawers, made from 1-by lumber and a project panel — a knockoff of a pricey retail table.
description:
This copies the look of an expensive parsons desk using nothing exotic: the fat square legs are three 1x3s laminated into a solid post, then wrapped with 1x3 and 1x4 trim so they read as thick, and the top is a project panel or a quarter-sheet of MDF. It's all glue and nails, so no advanced joinery — but there's real precision hiding in the drawers.

Build the legs carefully: the trim orientation isn't arbitrary. The 1x3 trim faces the front and back of the desk, the 1x4 trim faces the sides, and all four legs must be built consistently or the desk won't read square. Keep every outside edge flush as you laminate and wrap.

The drawers are where beginners get bitten. The drawer housing (boards H and I) must go in dead square — an out-of-square housing means drawers that bind and never slide right — and the drawer boxes themselves must be square, with squarely-cut plywood bottoms. Take the time to check both with a square. When you fit the faces, the overhang is intentionally uneven (7/8" on the inside, 1/2" on the outside), so mount the drawers on their slides, slide them in, and position the faces in place before fixing them.

tools.note: Optional — the build is mostly glue and nails.
Nice for ripping clean drawer parts, but not required.
The whole desk goes together with glue and 1-1/4"/2" nails.
Check the drawer housing and drawer boxes for square — this is where builds fail.

materials.name: 24" x 48" project panel or 3/4" MDF
materials.note: Tabletop. A quarter of an MDF sheet.

steps.title: Laminate the leg cores
steps.body: Build all four leg cores by nailing two A boards together flat-face to flat-face with 1-1/4" nails and glue, then a third A board on with 2" nails, keeping all outside edges flush. This makes the solid post the trim wraps around.

steps.title: Wrap the legs with trim
steps.body: Add trim board B to each core, flush at the outside and bottom, then board C over it fastening into all the sandwiched 1x3s. Build all four legs the same way: the 1x3 trim (B) faces front and back, the 1x4 trim (C) faces the sides. Consistency here is what keeps the desk looking square.

steps.title: Add the aprons
steps.body: Attach the side aprons (D) into the leg cores, keeping them flush with the outside edge of the leg centers so the desk is 24" wide. Then nail the front aprons (F) in, fastening into both the side aprons and the leg cores.

steps.title: Fasten the tabletop
steps.body: Set the project-panel or MDF top on the base, outside edges flush, and nail into all the side aprons, the back apron, and the leg pieces so it's fully anchored.

steps.title: Build the drawer housing — dead square
steps.body: Add the drawer housing boards H and I, driving a screw down through the tabletop into the top edge of the middle I board, and cross-nailing with plenty of glue — these carry the drawers. Get the housing exactly square; an out-of-square housing means drawers that bind forever.

steps.title: Build the drawers and fit the faces
steps.body: Nail the drawer sides (J) into the fronts/backs (K), checking each box for square, and add the squarely-cut 1/4" plywood bottoms (L) — a racked box won't slide. Mount the drawers on the slides and into the housing, then position the faces (M) with the intended uneven overhang (7/8" inside, 1/2" outside) before fixing them and adding pulls.

images.alt: Modern parsons-style desk with thick legs and two drawers
